title:
Patient Report
Redesign
subtitle: Redesigning the clinical patient report from a blank page into a structured, shareable communication tool.

slides.title:
Patient Report
Redesign
slides.description: After every scan, doctors needed to generate a report for the patient. The tool gave them a blank page. We redesigned it into a structured, template-driven system that produces professional reports in minutes.

slides.label: The Problem
slides.title: A blank page after every appointment
slides.content: The existing report tool had no structure, no templates, and no way to annotate or contextualise scan images. A third of the screen was always occupied by static information doctors couldn't act on while editing.
issues: Three-panel layout — right panel wasted 25% of screen space
Share and download buttons buried in a static sidebar
No templates — every report started from a blank page
Raw scan images with no annotation or tooth context
PDF output not designed to be read by patients
slides.highlight: The interface was designed around the tool — not around the doctor using it.

image.caption: Old — three-panel split, static sidebar, blank content area

slides.label: User Research
slides.title: What doctors told us
slides.content: We interviewed clinicians across multiple practices. The same problems surfaced everywhere — accepted as workarounds, not reported as issues.

quotes.text: I just skip the report most of the time. It takes too long.
quotes.author: Dr. A, General Practice

quotes.text: Patients ask me to explain things the report doesn't show.
quotes.author: Dr. B, Restorative

quotes.text: I have no way to circle what I'm talking about on the image.
quotes.author: Dr. C, Orthodontics

quotes.text: Every report looks different — there's no standard starting point.
quotes.author: Dr. D, Implantology

slides.label: Design Goals
slides.title: What we set out to fix.

goals.title: Give the canvas back to the doctor
goals.description: Move metadata and actions to the header — the full screen is for editing.

goals.title: Give doctors a starting point
goals.description: Templates tailored to common procedures — reports start structured, not blank.

goals.title: Make findings specific and visual
goals.description: Annotation and tooth tagging so doctors show exactly what they mean.

goals.title: Make sharing frictionless
goals.description: A report patients can receive and understand — with one-tap share options.

kpis: Time to create a complete report

slides.label: UI Layout
slides.title: From three fixed panels to a full editing canvas

afterImage.caption: New — compact header, full canvas for editing

slides.beforeDescription: The three-panel layout locked doctor info, preview, and share buttons into a permanent right sidebar — taking up 25% of the screen at all times.
slides.afterDescription: Everything moved to a compact top header — doctor, clinic, patient, and all actions inline. The full canvas is now the report.
beforeBullets: Right panel permanently occupied with static doctor/clinic info
Share and download buttons buried in the sidebar
Content area squeezed between two fixed panels
afterBullets: Doctor, clinic, and patient editable directly from the header
Share and Export PDF in the top bar — always one click away
Full screen dedicated to report content
slides.highlight: The header replaced an entire panel — and made every action faster to reach.

slides.label: Templates & Sections
slides.title: A structured starting point — built to flex
slides.content: Procedure-based templates pre-seed the report with the right sections. A modular block system lets doctors add, reorder, or remove anything to fit the case.
issues: Five templates — General Scan, Implant, Crown Prep, Follow-up, Custom
Each pre-seeded with the sections that procedure needs
Modular blocks — Image, Before/After, Clinical Details, Cost, Notes
Drag to reorder, one-tap duplicate or delete
slides.highlight: Templates give doctors a running start — sections let them stay in control.

image.caption: Template picker and modular section builder

slides.label: Annotation & Tooth Chart
slides.title: From plain images to clinically annotated findings

afterImage.caption: New — annotation canvas and FDI tooth chart

slides.beforeDescription: Images dropped in as-is — no way to mark findings or reference which teeth were involved.
slides.afterDescription: Doctors annotate directly on images and tag findings to specific teeth via an interactive FDI chart.
beforeBullets: No annotation — findings described in disconnected text
No tooth-level context — patients couldn't tell which teeth were affected
afterBullets: Pen, text, and color tools — annotate full-size before saving
Interactive FDI chart — click to tag teeth, shown as numbered pills in the report
slides.highlight: Two tools that answered the same question: where exactly is the problem?

slides.label: Before/After & Share
slides.title: Showing change — and getting it to the patient
slides.content: A dedicated before/after section lets doctors place two images side by side with custom labels. Once the report is ready, it's shared via a generated link — QR code, WhatsApp, SMS, or email — with an optional PIN so only the patient can open it.
bullets2: Before/After block — custom labels, optional comparison notes
QR code for in-chair handoff — no contact exchange needed
Optional 4–6 digit PIN shown in the share modal
slides.highlight: PDF export captures the full preview — clean typography, no raw images.

outcomes.title: Report creation
outcomes.description: Templates and a clear layout replaced blank-page setup — reports that took 15+ minutes now start in seconds.

outcomes.title: Clinical communication
outcomes.description: Annotation and tooth tagging let doctors show findings — not just describe them.

outcomes.title: Report completion rate
outcomes.description: With structure upfront, doctors stopped skipping the report step entirely.

outcomes.title: Reach per report
outcomes.description: Multi-channel sharing meant reports reached patients via their preferred channel.

slides.title: Thank You
slides.subtitle: Designing clinical tools that work at the speed of care.
